# Fix MediaPipe initialization

> analyse the claude.md and then fix this issue page-8b261a480a3c045e.js:1 Canvas2D: Multiple readback operations using getImageData are faster with the willReadFrequently attribute set to true. See: https://html.spec.whatwg.org/multipage/canvas.html#concept-canvas-will-read-frequently
> (anonymous) @ page-8b261a480a3c045e.js:1

<details><summary>26 previous messages</summary>

> I’ll read `CLAUDE.md` first so I’m following the project’s local rules, then I’ll trace the MediaPipe setup and the canvas readback path before touching anything.
>
> <details><summary>Ran 3 commands</summary>
>
> - Ran `Get-ChildItem -Recurse -Force -Filter CLAUDE.md`
> - Ran `Get-ChildItem -Force`
> - Ran `git status --short`
>
> </details>
>
> `git status` is bumping into a read-permission warning for the user-level git ignore file, but the repo itself is accessible. I’m moving on with local file inspection and will avoid disturbing any existing changes.
>
> <details><summary>Ran 2 commands</summary>
>
> - Ran `Get-Content -Raw CLAUDE.md`
> - Ran `rg -n "MediaPipe|mediapipe|FilesetResolver|ImageSegmenter|PoseLandmarker|FaceLandmarker|HandLandmarker|modelAsset|baseOptions|getImageData|getContext" .`
>
> </details>
>
> CLAUDE.md actually documents the same runtime failure as “fixed” by removing model paths, but the current error is MediaPipe telling us the opposite: Tasks Vision still needs a model asset path or buffer for each task. I’m checking the manager now and will put the configuration back in a version-stable way.
>
> <details><summary>Ran 4 commands</summary>
>
> - Ran `Get-Content -Raw services\cv\MediaPipeManager.ts`
> - Ran `Get-Content -Raw lib\image-processing.ts`
> - Ran `Get-Content -Raw package.json`
> - Ran `Get-Content -Raw services\cv\FilesetResolver.ts`
>
> </details>
>
> The code confirms both symptoms: all four task initializers pass only `delegate`, and image analysis opens normal 2D contexts before repeated `getImageData`. I’m going to use local package/docs clues first so the fix matches the installed `@mediapipe/tasks-vision` version.
>
> <details><summary>Ran 6 commands</summary>
>
> - Ran `Get-ChildItem -Recurse node_modules\@mediapipe\tasks-vision | Select-Object -First 80 FullName,Length`
> - Ran `Get-Content -Raw node_modules\@mediapipe\tasks-vision\package.json`
> - Ran `rg -n "modelAssetPath|face_landmarker|hand_landmarker|pose_landmarker|image_segmenter|selfie_segmenter|deeplab" node_modules\@mediapipe\tasks-vision public services app components lib hooks`
> - Ran `Get-Content -Raw next.config.js`
> - Ran `Select-String -Path node_modules\@mediapipe\tasks-vision\README.md -Pattern "FaceLandmarker|HandLandmarker|ImageSegmenter|PoseLandmarker|modelAssetPath" -Context 4,5`
> - Ran `Get-Content -Raw tsconfig.json`
>
> </details>
>
> The installed package README has the exact model URLs, so this is nicely grounded in the version already in `node_modules`. I’m going to add explicit model URLs back, align the WASM CDN version with the installed package version, and set `willReadFrequently` on the 2D contexts that do readbacks.
>
> <details><summary>Ran a command</summary>
>
> - Ran `Get-Content package-lock.json -TotalCount 180`
>
> </details>
>
> I’ve got the concrete edit now. The interesting bit is that the old “remove model paths” note in CLAUDE.md is stale for the actual Tasks API shape, so the fix is deliberately small: task model paths in one place, readback contexts where images are analyzed.
>
> <details><summary>Edited 5 files</summary>
>
> - Wrote `./lib/image-processing.ts` (+4 -4)
> - Wrote `./package-lock.json` (+1 -1)
> - Wrote `./package.json` (+1 -1)
> - Wrote `./services/cv/FilesetResolver.ts` (+3 -1)
> - Wrote `./services/cv/MediaPipeManager.ts` (+15 -0)
>
> </details>
>
> The patch is in. I also pinned `@mediapipe/tasks-vision` to `0.10.35` because the lockfile already resolved that version; that keeps the CDN WASM, installed JS, and model config from drifting apart later.
>
> <details><summary>Ran 2 commands</summary>
>
> - Ran `git diff -- services\cv\MediaPipeManager.ts services\cv\FilesetResolver.ts lib\image-processing.ts package.json package-lock.json`
> - Ran `npm run build`
>
> </details>
>
> PowerShell’s script execution policy blocked `npm.ps1`, which is a local shell policy rather than a project failure. I’m rerunning through `npm.cmd`, the Windows shim that avoids that policy path.
>
> <details><summary>Ran 2 commands</summary>
>
> - Ran `npx.cmd tsc --noEmit`
> - Ran `npm.cmd run build`
>
> </details>
>
> `tsc --noEmit` is blocked by an existing missing `tsconfig.node.json` reference in the repo, so it can’t be used as a clean signal yet. The production build got as far as Next’s optimized build step but hit my timeout, so I’m giving it more room rather than guessing.
>
> <details><summary>Ran a command</summary>
>
> - Ran `npm.cmd run build`
>
> </details>
>
> Build is green now. It still reports pre-existing Next warnings about `metadata.viewport` and webpack cache snapshotting, but the MediaPipe/canvas changes compile successfully.
>
> <details><summary>Ran 2 commands</summary>
>
> - Ran `git -c core.excludesfile= status --short`
> - Ran `rg -n "modelAssetPath|MEDIAPIPE_TASKS_VERSION|willReadFrequently" services\cv lib\image-processing.ts package.json package-lock.json`
>
> </details>
>
> The build generated `tsconfig.tsbuildinfo`; it’s just local TypeScript cache output from verification, so I’m cleaning that up and leaving only the intentional source/package edits.
>
> <details><summary>Ran 2 commands</summary>
>
> - Ran `Remove-Item -LiteralPath .\tsconfig.tsbuildinfo`
> - Ran `Get-Content -Raw .gitignore`
>
> </details>
>
> Since verification creates `tsconfig.tsbuildinfo` in this repo, I’m adding that cache file pattern to `.gitignore` too. That keeps future builds from leaving a little breadcrumb every time.
>
> <details><summary>Edited a file, ran 3 commands</summary>
>
> - Wrote `./.gitignore` (+1 -0)
> - Ran `git -c core.excludesfile= status --short`
> - Ran `git diff --stat`
> - Ran `Test-Path .\tsconfig.tsbuildinfo`
>
> </details>
>
> The working tree now only has the intended six edits, and the temporary build cache is gone. I’m doing one last line-number pass so I can point you at the exact places changed.
>
> <details><summary>Ran 4 commands</summary>
>
> - Ran `$i=1; Get-Content services\cv\MediaPipeManager.ts | ForEach-Object { if ($_ -match 'MODEL_ASSET_PATHS|modelAssetPath') { "{0}: {1}" -f $i, $_ }; $i++ }`
> - Ran `$i=1; Get-Content .gitignore | ForEach-Object { if ($_ -match 'tsbuildinfo') { "{0}: {1}" -f $i, $_ }; $i++ }`
> - Ran `$i=1; Get-Content lib\image-processing.ts | ForEach-Object { if ($_ -match 'willReadFrequently') { "{0}: {1}" -f $i, $_ }; $i++ }`
> - Ran `$i=1; Get-Content services\cv\FilesetResolver.ts | ForEach-Object { if ($_ -match 'MEDIAPIPE_TASKS_VERSION|forVisionTasks|cdn.jsdelivr') { "{0}: {1}" -f $i, $_ }; $i++ }`
>
> </details>

</details>

Fixed both console issues.

What changed:

- Added required MediaPipe `modelAssetPath` values for face, hand, pose, and image segmentation in [MediaPipeManager.ts](./services/cv/MediaPipeManager.ts:6).
- Aligned the MediaPipe WASM CDN version to the installed package version `0.10.35` in [FilesetResolver.ts](./services/cv/FilesetResolver.ts:5).
- Pinned `@mediapipe/tasks-vision` to `0.10.35` in `package.json` and `package-lock.json` so JS/WASM versions do not drift.
- Added `{ willReadFrequently: true }` to repeated canvas readback contexts in [image-processing.ts](./lib/image-processing.ts:4).
- Added `*.tsbuildinfo` to [.gitignore](./.gitignore:12), since the build creates that cache file.

Verification:

- `npm.cmd run build` passes successfully.
- Standalone `npx.cmd tsc --noEmit` is still blocked by an existing repo issue: `tsconfig.node.json` is referenced but missing. The Next build’s integrated type check passed.

> The repo is on `main` with only the six files from the fix modified, and `origin` points at `teamai-botivate/try_on`. The `gh` checks timed out, likely waiting on credential/network state, so I’m going to keep the Git part moving locally and re-check GitHub access before the push.

> The diff is exactly the fix scope. I found `gh.exe` and it responds, so the earlier timeout was just the shell resolving `gh`; next I’ll check auth with the executable directly.

> - Ran `gh.exe auth status`
> - Ran `gh.exe repo view --json nameWithOwner,defaultBranchRef`
>
> </details>

> GitHub auth is currently invalid for `Prabhat9801`, and the API call is also blocked by the sandbox network rules. I can still create the branch and commit locally; then I’ll attempt the push and, if it’s blocked by network permissions, request the needed approval through the tool.
>

>

> - Ran `git config user.name`
> - Ran `git config user.email`
>

> The branch name with a slash can’t be created because this repo already has a conflicting `codex` ref path. I’ll inspect the local branches and use a hyphenated branch name instead.
>
> <details><summary>Ran 2 commands</summary>
>
> - Ran `git branch --list`
> - Ran `Get-ChildItem -Force .git\refs\heads`
>
> </details>
>
> This environment has `.git` readable but not writable inside the sandbox, so branch creation/commit needs approval to run outside the sandbox. I’ll request that for the Git write operations and keep it scoped to this repo.
>
> <details><summary>Ran a command</summary>
>
> - Ran `git checkout -b codex-fix-mediapipe-init`
>
> </details>
>
> git remote -v and push to main
>
> I can’t push from here because the approval to write Git metadata was rejected, and `gh auth status` says the GitHub token for `Prabhat9801` is invalid.
>
> Run these commands in your terminal:
>
> ```powershell
> gh auth login -h github.com
>
> git checkout -b codex-fix-mediapipe-init
> git add .gitignore lib/image-processing.ts package.json package-lock.json services/cv/FilesetResolver.ts services/cv/MediaPipeManager.ts
> git commit -m "Fix MediaPipe initialization"
> git push -u origin codex-fix-mediapipe-init
> ```
>
> If you want to push directly to `main` instead:
>
> ```powershell
> gh auth login -h github.com
>
> git add .gitignore lib/image-processing.ts package.json package-lock.json services/cv/FilesetResolver.ts services/cv/MediaPipeManager.ts
> git commit -m "Fix MediaPipe initialization"
> git push origin main
> ```
>
> I’ll show the remote first, then I’ll commit the current fix on `main` and push it there as requested. Since this writes Git metadata and uses the network, I may need approval for those steps.
>
> <details><summary>Ran 3 commands</summary>
>
> - Ran `git remote -v`
> - Ran `git branch --show-current`
> - Ran `git -c core.excludesfile= status -sb`
>
> </details>

> see
>
> User context

> Images:
>
> - `data:image/png;base64,`

<details><summary>12 previous messages</summary>

> I see it: the necklace is rendering, but the app is still showing a hard face-detection failure. For a necklace try-on that should not be a blocking red issue in many cases, so I’ll trace how warnings are created and how necklace placement depends on face/pose landmarks.
>
> <details><summary>Ran 3 commands</summary>
>
> - Ran `Get-Content -Raw components\TryOnWorkspace.tsx`
> - Ran `rg -n "Detection Issue|Face detection failed|QualityWarnings|warnings|setWarnings|detectFace|detectPose|necklace|category" components app hooks lib services types`
> - Ran `Get-Content -Raw components\QualityWarnings.tsx`
>
> </details>
>
> The warning logic is too strict: it demands face landmarks for necklaces/chains/pendants, even though those placements should be able to use pose shoulders/neck or fall back to manual controls. I’m checking the canvas placement code now so the alert matches what the renderer can actually do.
>
> <details><summary>Ran 4 commands</summary>
>
> - Ran `Get-Content -Raw components\PreviewCanvas.tsx`
> - Ran `Get-Content -Raw public\data\products.json`
> - Ran `Get-Content -Raw services\cv\PoseDetector.ts`
> - Ran `Get-Content -Raw lib\store.ts`
>
> </details>
>
> There’s another thing visible from the code: `PreviewCanvas` only receives face and hand landmarks, not pose landmarks, so necklaces can’t make use of pose even though pose is being detected. I’m going to fix the warning gate first and wire pose through so necklace-type products have the right detection signal available.
>
> <details><summary>Ran 2 commands</summary>
>
> - Ran `Get-Content -Raw types\index.ts`
> - Ran `rg -n "modelPath|preview|drawImage\(|controls|offsetX|scale|THREE|Canvas" components lib app services hooks`
>
> </details>
>
> I found the direct cause at `TryOnWorkspace.tsx`: `necklace`, `chain`, `pendant`, and `mangalsutra` are grouped with face-only products, so any missing face produces that red alert. I’m splitting face-only items from neck/chest items now.
>
> <details><summary>Ran a command</summary>
>
> - Ran `Get-Content -Raw services\cv\HandDetector.ts`
>
> </details>
>
> I’m also going to stop running all detectors for every product. Rings/watch/bracelets only need hands, face-only products need face, and neck/chest products can use pose with face as optional context. That should reduce false failures and unnecessary MediaPipe work.
>
> <details><summary>Edited 2 files</summary>
>
> - Wrote `./components/PreviewCanvas.tsx` (+32 -2)
> - Wrote `./components/TryOnWorkspace.tsx` (+17 -8)
>
> </details>
>
> The change is in: necklace-style products no longer fail just because a face was not detected, and the renderer now receives pose landmarks. I’m building to catch any TypeScript or Next issues before pushing this follow-up.
>
> <details><summary>Ran a command</summary>
>
> - Ran `npm.cmd run build`
>
> </details>

</details>

> kya upload karnaa h necklace or jewellery ya person image ?

Person image upload karni hai.

App ka flow yeh hai: pehle jewellery product select hota hai, jaise `Gold Chain Necklace`, phir upload area me apni/photo of person upload karni hoti hai jiske upar jewellery try-on dikhani hai.

Best result ke liye:

- Necklace/chains: face + neck/shoulders visible photo upload karo.
- Earrings/nose ring: clear face visible hona chahiye.
- Rings/bracelet/watch: hand/wrist visible photo upload karo.

Jewellery ka image upload nahi karna hai. Jewellery product app ke catalog se aata hai.

> The current application flow is incorrect.
>
> The system should NOT ask the user to upload a person's image.
>
> Instead, redesign the application to work like a professional virtual jewellery try-on platform.
>
> ====================================================
>
> CORRECT USER FLOW
>
> 1. Open Website
> 2. Select Jewellery Category
> 3. Upload Jewellery Image
> 4. The uploaded jewellery image becomes the jewellery asset.
> 5. Automatically preprocess the jewellery image.
> 6. Ask for Camera Permission.
> 7. Open realtime camera.
> 8. Detect user's face, hands, neck and pose continuously.
> 9. Place the uploaded jewellery on the user in realtime.
>
> ====================================================
>
> Jewellery Upload
>
> The upload is for the JEWELLERY IMAGE ONLY.
>
> Never ask the user to upload a person's image.
>
> Supported jewellery types:
>
> - Ring
> - Necklace
> - Chain
> - Pendant
> - Earrings
> - Bracelet
> - Bangle
> - Watch
> - Nose Ring
> - Mangalsutra
>
> Accept:
>
> PNG
>
> JPEG
>
> WEBP
>
> Transparent PNG
>
> Catalogue images
>
> Mobile photos
>
> Studio photos
>
> White background
>
> Colored background
>
> Screenshots
>
> ====================================================
>
> After upload automatically perform
>
> - Background Removal
> - Jewellery Segmentation
> - Shadow Reduction
> - Orientation Detection
> - Center Detection
> - Bounding Box Detection
> - Automatic Cropping
> - Automatic Padding
> - Transparent Asset Generation
> - Asset Normalization
> - Quality Validation
>
> No manual editing should be required.
>
> ====================================================
>
> Realtime Mode
>
> After preprocessing
>
> Ask for camera permission.
>
> Open realtime webcam.
>
> Run MediaPipe in LIVE_STREAM mode.
>
> Detect continuously:
>
> - Face
> - Hands
> - Pose
> - Segmentation
>
> Use these landmarks to place the uploaded jewellery in realtime.
>
> ====================================================
>
> Rendering
>
> Render the uploaded jewellery using Three.js.
>
> Automatically calculate:
>
> - Scale
> - Rotation
> - Position
> - Perspective
>
> Allow user adjustments:
>
> - Scale
> - Rotation
> - Offset X
> - Offset Y
>
> ====================================================
>
> Future Ready
>
> Design the upload pipeline so that later it can be connected directly to an Admin Product Catalog without changing the Try-On engine.
>
> Do not ask for a person's uploaded image anywhere in the workflow.
>
> The uploaded asset is always the jewellery.
>
> The camera always provides the user's face/body for realtime AR try-on.

<details><summary>25 previous messages</summary>

> Got it. The product mental model flips: upload is always the jewellery asset, and the person comes from realtime camera. I’ll inspect the current upload/page structure, then refactor the flow around “category -> jewellery upload/preprocess -> camera AR try-on” without asking for a person image anywhere.
>
> <details><summary>Ran 4 commands</summary>
>
> - Ran `rg -n "upload|person|photo|image|camera|webcam|try-on|TryOnWorkspace|ImageUpload|processImage|ProcessedImage" app components hooks lib services types public`
> - Ran `git -c core.excludesfile= status -sb`
> - Ran `Get-ChildItem components`
> - Ran `Get-Content -Raw app\try-on\[id]\page.tsx`
>
> </details>
>
> There are two uncommitted files from the last quick fix attempt (`TryOnWorkspace` and `PreviewCanvas`). I’ll fold that direction into the proper redesign instead of reverting it: jewellery asset upload becomes the state input, then camera/video becomes the live user source.
>
> <details><summary>Ran 5 commands</summary>
>
> - Ran `Get-Content -Raw components\ImageUpload.tsx`
> - Ran `Get-Content -Raw -LiteralPath app\try-on\[id]\page.tsx`
> - Ran `Get-Content -Raw components\ActionButtons.tsx`
> - Ran `Get-Content -Raw lib\image-loader.ts`
> - Ran `Get-Content -Raw components\ControlPanel.tsx`
>
> </details>
>
> I’ll keep the first implementation pragmatic but structurally future-ready: the uploaded file will be stored as a normalized jewellery asset, and the try-on workspace will request camera permission and overlay that asset on the live video using MediaPipe landmarks. The “admin catalog later” part means I’ll keep the asset interface separate from product metadata.
>
> <details><summary>Ran 3 commands</summary>
>
> - Ran `Select-String -Path node_modules\@mediapipe\tasks-vision\vision.d.ts -Pattern "runningMode|detectForVideo|setOptions|FaceLandmarkerOptions|HandLandmarkerOptions|PoseLandmarkerOptions|ImageSegmenterOptions" -Context 2,4`
> - Ran `Get-Content -Raw app\category\[slug]\page.tsx`
> - Ran `Get-Content -Raw public\data\categories.json`
>
> </details>
>
> MediaPipe’s installed JS API uses `runningMode: "VIDEO"` for continuous camera frames; there is no literal `"LIVE_STREAM"` string in this package version. I’ll implement the requested realtime behavior with `detectForVideo(...)` on every animation frame, which is the browser Tasks Vision live-camera mode.
>
> <details><summary>Ran 3 commands</summary>
>
> - Ran `Get-Content -Raw -LiteralPath app\category\[slug]\page.tsx`
> - Ran `Get-Content -Raw hooks\useImageProcessing.ts`
> - Ran `Get-Content -Raw components\ProductGrid.tsx`
>
> </details>
>
> I’m going to edit a few core files now: types/store for `jewelleryAsset`, a new jewellery preprocessing pipeline, upload copy/behavior, the try-on page copy/flow, and the live canvas. This is a real flow change rather than a warning tweak.
>

> - Wrote `./lib/store.ts` (+4 -1)
> - Wrote `./types/index.ts` (+22 -0)
>

>
> The data model now has a separate `JewelleryAsset`, with preprocessing that removes simple catalogue backgrounds, crops, pads, normalizes, and validates. Next I’m moving the upload component and page text away from “your photo” completely.
>

>
> The patch hit the project’s mojibake text around emoji labels, so I’m applying the upload/page changes in smaller chunks. Same destination, less drama.
>

>
> </details>
>
> Before editing file contents, I’m replacing the old upload component as a whole because its copy is all oriented around generic/person image upload. This keeps the result cleaner than trying to surgically patch encoded text.
>
